%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Inverse z-Transform by Power Series Expansion

> **Section Objective:** Learn how to recover the first samples of a causal sequence by expanding \(X[z]\) as a power series in \(z^{-1}\).

---

## Concepts In This Section

- z-transform as a power series
- coefficient matching
- long division in powers of \(z^{-1}\)
- first samples versus closed form

## 1. The z-transform is already a coefficient list

This definition is the engine behind the entire method. For a causal sequence, \(X[z]\) is a power series in \(z^{-1}\): each term \(x[n]z^{-n}\) pairs one time-domain sample with one basis element.

**Symbol guide:** \(X[z]\) is the z-transform, \(x[n]\) is the sample at time index \(n\), \(z^{-n}\) is the basis term, and \(n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\)

**When to use it:** whenever an exam asks for the inverse z-transform by power series expansion, or asks only for the first few samples.

**Exam trigger:** the phrase "expand in powers of \(z^{-1}\)".

**Common misuse:** expanding in positive powers of \(z\) and then reading those coefficients as \(x[n]\) — this gives the wrong sequence entirely.

**Quick example:** the coefficient of \(z^{-2}\) is \(x[2]\).

$$X[z]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}x[n]\,z^{-n}$$

## 2. How long division creates the power series

This is the practical reading form — not a new formula, just the series written out term by term.

After expansion, do not solve another equation. Simply match the coefficient beside each power of \(z^{-1}\):

- Constant term \(\rightarrow x[0]\)
- Coefficient of \(z^{-1}\) \(\rightarrow x[1]\)
- Coefficient of \(z^{-2}\) \(\rightarrow x[2]\)

**Common trap:** thinking the coefficient of \(z^{-1}\) gives \(x[-1]\). It does not. The basis term is \(z^{-n}\), so \(z^{-1}\) corresponds to \(n=1\), not \(n=-1\). The exponent is \(-n\), and solving \(-n = -1\) gives \(n = 1\).

$$X[z]=x[0]+x[1]z^{-1}+x[2]z^{-2}+x[3]z^{-3}+\cdots$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Read the inverse samples directly

For a rational \(X[z]\), polynomial long division is a mechanical procedure that rewrites the fraction as a sequence of terms in descending powers \(1, z^{-1}, z^{-2}, z^{-3}, \ldots\)

The numerator is divided by the denominator. The critical requirement is that the output must be organized in powers of \(z^{-1}\) — only then can each coefficient be read directly as a time-domain sample.

**Minimal concrete step:** if the quotient begins \(7 + 9.9z^{-1}\), then the first two samples are already identified: \(x[0] = 7\) and \(x[1] = 9.9\). No further equation-solving is needed.

### COMMON EXAM MISTAKE

Stopping after algebraic simplification without rewriting the result as a \(z^{-1}\)-series. The coefficients are only readable once the expression is fully expanded in powers of \(z^{-1}\).

$$X[z]=\frac{z^{2}(7z-2)}{(z-0.2)(z-0.5)(z-1)}=7+9.9z^{-1}+11.23z^{-2}+11.87z^{-3}+\cdots$$
*This is the section's main worked example. The rational expression on the left is not being solved for a closed-form sequence — it is being expanded so the first coefficients become directly visible.

**Symbol guide:** \(X[z]\) is the z-transform; the right-hand side is the quotient series produced by long division; each numerical coefficient belongs to one power of \(z^{-1}\) and equals one time-domain sample.

**Exam trigger:** a rational \(X[z]\) paired with the instruction "use long division" or "find the first few values of \(x[n]\)".

**Common misuse:** seeing the denominator factors \((z-0.2)(z-0.5)(z-1)\) and immediately setting up partial fractions. When the problem specifies long division or power-series expansion, partial fractions are not the requested method — even if they would also work.*

$$x[0]=7,\quad x[1]=9.9,\quad x[2]=11.23,\quad x[3]=11.87,\quad \ldots$$
*This is the inverse z-transform information delivered by the power-series method: a list of samples, one per coefficient.

**Limitation:** long division is excellent when only the first few samples are needed. It usually does not reveal a clean closed-form expression for all \(n\). For example, a simple geometric case like \(X[z] = z/(z-0.5)\) will produce coefficients \(1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, \ldots\) that suggest the pattern \((0.5)^n u[n]\), but the long-division method itself only produces the list — recognizing the closed form requires a separate step.

When a closed-form \(x[n]\) is required, a different inverse z-transform method is more appropriate.

**Common trap:** writing only the list of numbers \(7, 9.9, 11.23, 11.87\) without labeling each number with its index \(n\). Always write \(x[0] = 7\), \(x[1] = 9.9\), etc.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- The z-transform of a causal sequence is a power series: $$X[z]=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}x[n]\,z^{-n}$$ Each coefficient \(x[n]\) multiplies the basis term \(z^{-n}\).
- The expanded reading form is $$X[z]=x[0]+x[1]z^{-1}+x[2]z^{-2}+\cdots$$ Match the coefficient beside each power of \(z^{-1}\) directly to the sample at that index. The exponent is \(-n\), so \(z^{-1}\) gives \(x[1]\), not \(x[-1]\).
- For a rational \(X[z]\), polynomial long division in powers of \(z^{-1}\) produces the series. The textbook example gives $$X[z]=7+9.9z^{-1}+11.23z^{-2}+11.87z^{-3}+\cdots$$
- Reading the coefficients directly yields the first samples: \(x[0]=7\), \(x[1]=9.9\), \(x[2]=11.23\), \(x[3]=11.87\). The method is ideal for the first few samples but usually does not produce a compact closed-form \(x[n]\).

*Next, you will use other z-transform tools when a closed-form inverse sequence is needed.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
